That's just the start of a very long list, says Delaplane: Bees are an integral part not only in food productionbut also otherareaswe might neverconsider- suchas textile manufacturing(bees pollinate cotton crops). Without bees to do their jobs producing more plants for dyes,[8] our clothing options would be bland. And that's justone of many examples, says Delaplane; thelist goeson and includes"so much stuffin yourdaily life you take completely for granted." What is being done about it? texts.full_text: tation Now About SAEN About Michael Budkie About SAEN: Leading the Fight Stop Animal Exploitation NOW! (SAEN) was founded in 1996 to force an end to the abuse of animals in laboratories. Our first major event was a news conference that revealed the suffering endured by dogs, rabbits, and primates in 9 laboratories across the United States. Documents obtained by SAEN had revealed severe abuses within the laboratories of Michigan State University, University of Southern California, University of Washington, Stolle Research and Development, University of South Florida, Yerkes Regional Primate Research Center (associated with Emory University), University of Florida, University of Pennsylvania, and the University of Toledo. The USDA fined several of these laboratories, and abusive primate experiments within the laboratories were halted as a direct result. Since its inception, SAEN has been at the forefront of advocating for ethical alternatives to animal testing and exposing cruel practices in laboratory settings. Through their relentless pursuit of justice and compassion towards animals used in scientific research, they have initiated numerous campaigns that bring attention to unethical treatment within laboratories across America. In addition to raising awareness through news conferences, investigations into questionable experimental methods continue regularly under SAEN’s scrutiny. By gathering evidence from animal caretakers who are willing or forced to report mistreatment firsthand and by reviewing publicly accessible records pertaining to laboratory protocols compliance issues can arise which often lead to legal actions taken against institutions involved in illegal practices. The organization also plays a critical role within legislative processes aimed at reforming regulations governing experimentation on animals. By lobbying governmental entities, providing expert testimonies before lawmakers during committee hearings on animal welfare bills and educating policymakers about the necessity of transitioning away from invasive techniques towards non-animal alternatives wherever possible—SAEN ensures that ethical considerations remain central in ongoing scientific progress. Moreover, SAEN collaborates with other like-minded organizations worldwide to build a global network advocating for better treatment standards within laboratory practices. This collective action contributes significantly toward advancing public sentiment concerning the importance of animal rights an

texts.full_text: puzzled thinkers for centuries. From the ancient Greeks to the Enlightenment, the debate has evolved, integrating both philosophical speculation and scientific inquiry. Genz delves into these discussions, examining the contributions of key figures and the shifts in understanding. He highlights the transition from philosophical musings to the empirical evidence of quantum mechanics, which reveals the dynamic nature of the vacuum. This includes pre-Platoian times, Plato, Aristotle, the Scholastic philosophers, Galileo, newton, Leibniz, Descartes, and the modern scientists who have left us with experimental evidence that we are really dealing with a quantum mechanical vacuum filled with fluctuations. Although the history of these ideas is important and frequently interesting Genz can go beyond what many people are expecting or wanting to read. Chapter 2, for instance,

Loading content ... THE MAROON WWW.LOYOLAMAROON.COM FRIDAY, OCTOBER 31, 2008 VOL. 86, NO. 7 THEY GAME FROM OUTER SPACE! By MASAKO HIRSCH STAFF WRITER Loyola beamed up a number of new art pieces placed around campus to complement the city-wide international modern art show called Prospect I . The exhibit is on display until January and features works by artists from all over the world who have been commissioned to create site specific installations. “The idea was that we would be able to bring in some work, but it had to fit into our campus,” said Loyola’s Director of Art Galleries Dan Cameron. “We wanted something that could go outside.” The art pieces are located on St. Charles Avenue and the Quad. One piece is a large sculpture by artist Mark Handforth called Untitled (2008). It consists of two giant hands holding up an enormous light bulb, which sits in front of Monroe Hall. Another installation features 15 sculptures made from recycled materials that were placed around campus on Oct. 9 and will remain until the end of Prospect I . The pieces are by artist Willie Birch who is a New Orleans native. “The idea was to have them be like little aliens,” Cameron said, referring to how they look as if they came from outer space.
